Promising Alzheimer’s therapies have stumbled in clinical trials, billions of dollars have been spent chasing elusive biological targets and, despite recent progress, researchers are still wrestling with difficult realities. articles.summary: NanoDetection secures global rights to antibody technology that may help distinguish Alzheimer’s from other brain diseases. A new licensing deal aims to bring greater precision to one of medicine’s biggest challenges: understanding what is happening inside the aging brain. articles.summary: Rokit Healthcare announced it has received clinical approval for an advanced regenerative medicine therapy for kidney regeneration and plans to begin the world’s first human clinical surgery in July 2026. The clinical trial will evaluate a kidney regeneration platform using autologous omentum-derived cells and will assess the initial safety and regenerative potential of an omentum […] The post Rokit Healthcare to begin human kidney regeneration surgery in July appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.summary: Tectonic Therapeutic has completed enrollment in the APEX Phase 2 trial evaluating TX45 in patients with pulmonary hypertension associated with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (PH-HFpEF). The 24-week, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial enrolled 191 patients across 14 countries and was enriched for combined pre- and post-capillary pulmonary hypertension (CpcPH); 137 patients had a baseline […] The post Tectonic Therapeutic finishes patient enrollment for trial of new heart failure drug appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

The patent is expected to provide protection in South Korea through 2042. articles.summary: Heartseed has begun dosing patients in its domestic Phase I/II EMERALD clinical trial of HS-005, an allogeneic induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocyte spheroid therapy delivered via catheter for heart failure caused by dilated cardiomyopathy. The Independent Safety Monitoring Committee reviewed four-week post-administration data from the first patient and cleared continuation of the dilated cardiomyopathy cohort, […] The post Heartseed tests stem cell therapy for dilated cardiomyopathy patients appeared first on Longevity.Technology .

articles.title: Could leaves help feed humanity after disaster?
articles.summary: UC researchers are investigating whether leaf protein and sugar extracted from plant fiber could help sustain people if major global shocks disrupt food production. Te Whare Wānanga o Waitaha | University of Canterbury (UC) Associate Professor David Denkenberger, who has spent more than a decade studying food resilience in extreme scenarios, says the work is part of a broader effort to identify practical ways to prevent mass starvation if global food systems are severely disrupted.

articles.title: New atlas reveals more about how the body's 'master gland' really works
articles.summary: A new study has created a detailed map of the pituitary gland, often called the body's "master gland" because it controls important functions such as growth, stress and reproduction. Researchers from the Center for Craniofacial & Regenerative Biology combined data from many studies to build a single, clearer picture of how this gland works. They created the Consensus Pituitary Atlas, along with an easy-to-use website where scientists can explore the data and analyze their own.

articles.title: Engineering enzymes with potential against ALS and Parkinson's disease
articles.summary: In an advance that could one day lead to new treatments for neurodegenerative diseases, Meredith Jackrel, an associate professor of chemistry in Arts & Sciences at Washington University in St. Louis, and her team have developed a method to rapidly produce and screen a class of disaggregase enzymes that can break down the misfolded proteins associated with ALS and Parkinson's disease.

articles.title: Lab-grown canine muscle cells offer solution for early therapeutic testing
articles.summary: Before testing new therapies in animals, researchers now have a more efficient starting point—lab-grown canine muscle cells that can help identify what works and what doesn't.

articles.title: Molecular anchors on gut phages could open new therapeutic avenues
articles.summary: Bacteriophages, or phages, are viruses that infect bacteria and are not considered human pathogens. Yet researchers at the Translational Microbiology Laboratory of the Institute of Biochemistry, HUN-REN Biological Research Centre, Szeged, have shown that some gut phages can also physically interact with human cells. Their study identifies phage surface proteins that act as molecular anchors, promoting attachment to human cells, cellular uptake, and prolonged retention in the gastrointestinal tract. The findings, published in the journal Nature Communications, open new perspectives on how phages behave in the body and may create new opportunities for therapeutic development.

articles.title: Engineered bacterium turns potato starch into biodegradable plastic in 24 hours
articles.summary: Every year, hundreds of millions of tons of petrochemical-based plastics are produced, much of which ends up in the environment or is incinerated. This exacerbates greenhouse gas emissions and the environmental crisis caused by plastic pollution. Now, a study led by the University of Barcelona has produced a biodegradable bioplastic of high industrial value—polyhydroxybutyrate, or PHB—from unprocessed potato starch in a single 24-hour step, a strategic breakthrough that could help reduce dependence on oil and the volume of persistent plastic waste.

articles.title: AI fast-forwards molecular simulations by 10,000-fold
articles.summary: A new AI model has become so good at predicting how molecules evolve over time that, in the future, it could speed up the costly and time-consuming process of testing new drugs. In the long term, this technology could facilitate the development of medicines and new treatments, as promising drug candidates can be identified more quickly and with greater accuracy.

articles.title: Ocean glow meets 3D printing with living gels that sense mechanical force
articles.summary: The integration of biological organisms into synthetic structures offers a radical new pathway for developing intelligent, self-powered materials. Researchers have pioneered an innovative approach to biomanufacturing by using light-based 3D printing to engineer living material systems capable of localized environmental processing.

articles.title: Researchers develop a new anti-jellyfish floating buoy
articles.summary: Researchers from the Universitat Politècnica de València (UPV) and the University of Alicante have developed a new anti-jellyfish floating buoy that helps prevent these creatures from reaching the coast, thereby reducing the risk of stings among bathers.

articles.title: Spatial CRISPR screens map total RNA in tissue
articles.summary: Nature Biotechnology, Published online: 11 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41587-026-03126-z A method for spatial CRISPR perturbation screening reveals how genetic changes alter coding and non-coding RNA in a native tissue context, including the tumor microenvironment.

articles.title: Venice and Morpheus tokens climb as US ban on Anthropic’s Fable 5 fuels ‘permissionless AI’ pitch
articles.summary: Venice founder Erik Voorhees and the official Morpheus account seized on the shutdown, casting it as proof of "permissionless" AI's use case.

articles.title: Coinbase quantum report flags exchange cold wallets among millions of bitcoin exposed by address reuse
articles.summary: The report lays out possible solutions to the abandoned coins problem, such as setting a deadline for migration and then freezing vulnerable coins.

articles.title: Crypto’s next billion-dollar hacker may move at superhuman speed
articles.summary: Anthropic’s new Claude Fable 5 puts powerful cyber tools behind safety filters. DeFi, already hit by more than $840 million in hacks this year, is one of the industries with the most to lose if the filters fail.

articles.title: Here's what SpaceX's IPO means for its $1.3 billion bitcoin reserve
articles.summary: The largest company on public markets now holds bitcoin as a treasury reserve, not as a business model. Its first earnings cycles will test which version of corporate crypto survives a bear market.

articles.title: Stablecoins Were Meant to Disrupt Finance. Instead, They Became Idle Cash.
articles.summary: O’Connor argues that crypto’s clearest success story has scaled as money but not as capital.

articles.title: Bitcoin rises above $64,000 after Pakistan prime minister says Iran peace deal is near
articles.summary: Bitcoin traded above $64,000 on Saturday, supported by its strongest ETF inflows in a month and growing optimism around geopolitical developments.

articles.title: Wall Street is moving past crypto pilots and deeper into Ethereum, says Etherealize founder
articles.summary: In an interview with CoinDesk, Etherealize cofounder Vivek Raman said Ethereum is currently in a transitional phase where the infrastructure has largely been built, but the scale of adoption has yet to be fully reflected in ETH itself.

articles.title: Tokenization mirrors the $20 trillion ETF boom as blockchain and AI converge, Ondo exec says
articles.summary: Tokenization is laying the groundwork for autonomous investing and real-time portfolio management, Ondo's new head of portfolio products John Hoffman said.

articles.summary: BlackRock's IBIT led Friday's inflows at $57.7 million, with Fidelity's FBTC adding $18.0 million, while no fund reported a net outflow on the day.

articles.title: Anthropic's Mythos AI finds no more 'serious' bugs in Zcash: Wilcox
articles.summary: Zcash founder Zooko Wilcox said Anthropic's Mythos AI model found no further "serious bugs" in the privacy protocol following the patching of a previously discovered forgery bug.
